title: Fine-Tuning Humanoid Vision And Movement
gist: Humanoid robots are borrowing vision, safety, and zonal architectures directly from autonomous vehicles, with vision and mobility most mature but touch, whole-body reasoning, power efficiency, and future smell/taste sensing still the hard unsolved problems.

insights.claim: Humanoid robot system architecture is largely derived from autonomous vehicles, sharing zonal control, functional safety (FuSa) for microcontrollers, special Ethernet, and motor control.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Automotive semiconductor suppliers have a direct, reusable entry into the humanoid market via existing zonal/FuSa IP.
insights.quote: The system-level architecture of a robot is largely derived from autonomous vehicles.
insights.locator: The system-level architecture of a robot

insights.claim: Chip designers are starting robotics chip designs in automotive because the two are so overlapped, sustaining themselves on automotive channel revenue while betting nascent robotics startups will hit mass production.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Robotics silicon is a call option funded by automotive cash flow; supplier risk is low because chips are already in the auto supply chain.
insights.quote: In the meantime, the companies designing those chips are sustaining themselves through automotive channels.
insights.locator: There’s a whole range of robotics startups

insights.claim: Getting a viable robotics company to mass production could take five years because it must solve mechanical challenges plus AI software challenges, but chip suppliers embedded early lock into that eventual supply chain.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Humanoid mass-production is a multi-year horizon; near-term robotics chip revenue is speculative, favoring diversified suppliers.
insights.quote: It could take five years because the company’s got to solve all sorts of mechanical challenges, as well as the AI software challenges
insights.locator: There’s a whole range of robotics startups

insights.claim: Using gallium nitride for humanoid motors can reduce the size of the joints and save battery life.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: GaN adoption in robot actuators is a concrete design win for wide-bandgap suppliers targeting the humanoid BOM.
insights.quote: We can reduce the size of the joints by using gallium nitride for the motors.
insights.locator: You can break down the robots into many elements

insights.claim: Nvidia recently announced a full-stack, open robotics safety system as an extension of its Halos autonomous-vehicle safety framework.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Nvidia is extending AV safety IP into robotics, signaling platform lock-in ambitions across physical AI.
insights.locator: Mobility arguably has the furthest to go

insights.claim: Vehicle OEMs layer radar onto cameras to compensate for poor night/low-light camera performance, add lidar in certain vehicle classes, and are introducing thermal sensing for nighttime redundancy — a sensor stack humanoids will also need.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Sensor-fusion BOM (radar + lidar + thermal + camera) expands per-robot silicon and sensor content beyond cameras alone.
insights.locator: Most vehicle OEMs also integrate radar sensing

insights.claim: Humanoids will use multiple cameras at different resolutions and compute levels — wide low-fidelity fields of view for continuous perception plus targeted high-resolution capture — because matching human foveal vision with one camera is impractical for power reasons.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Multi-camera, multi-fidelity designs multiply image-sensor and vision-processor demand and create a tiered-compute design pattern.
insights.quote: we’re going to see multiple cameras and multiple compute levels
insights.locator: Even if processors could run all images

insights.claim: Robots are being fitted with electromagnetic radar sensors alongside optical cameras precisely because radar can see when cameras are blinded, such as driving toward the sun.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Radar-for-safety redundancy is migrating from cars into robots, sustaining automotive-radar suppliers' addressable market.
insights.quote: electromagnetic waves can see. So we’re seeing robots with optical sensors, as well as electromagnetic sensors.
insights.locator: Humanoids borrow vision safety features

insights.claim: ASIL-certified radar can create a fail-safe safety bubble around a robot to detect humans and children accurately even in dark, smoky, dusty, or high-speed conditions where cameras fail.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Safety certification (ASIL) is a differentiator for radar in human-proximate robots — a moat for auto-grade sensor vendors.
insights.quote: ASIL-certified radar devices can create a safety bubble around the robot
insights.locator: Most OEMs are mainly using cameras

insights.claim: A major unsolved sensor challenge is synchronizing many heterogeneous sensor types (cameras, depth, tactile, audio, force/torque, joint-position) and fusing them with low, predictable latency, accurate timestamping, and assured data integrity.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Sensor synchronization and data-movement standards (MIPI) are a critical enabling layer and standardization opportunity for physical AI.
insights.quote: One major challenge is synchronizing the many different sensor types used within humanoids
insights.locator: There are many sensor challenges

themes.theme: Movement, kinematics and the physical-AI problem

insights.claim: Natural humanoid movement requires computing forward kinematics, inverse kinematics, distance, and depth in real time, but the goal is intuitive human-like 'constant intelligence' rather than explicit XYZ calculation — the core physical-AI challenge yet to be cracked.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: The move from explicit kinematics to learned intuitive control is the key technical inflection gating general-purpose robots.
insights.quote: That’s constant intelligence, and that is what we want to crack right now. That is physical AI.
insights.locator: If I want a robot to pick something up

insights.claim: Touch, whole-body reasoning, and mobility in unstructured environments remain the hardest problems, while vision, speech, and basic manipulation are the most mature because they benefit from the existing AI and sensor ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Capability-maturity map tells investors where the durable technical gaps (and startup opportunities) still lie in humanoids.
insights.quote: The harder problems are touch, whole-body reasoning, and mobility in unstructured environments
insights.locator: Mobility arguably has the furthest to go

insights.claim: Humanoid hands may contain up to 30 actuators, and actuators are the majority of a robot's expensive hardware alongside BMS and power interfaces.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Actuator count and cost dominate the humanoid BOM — actuator/motor-driver suppliers capture disproportionate value.
insights.quote: Especially in the hands, there may be up to 30 actuators in the hands

insights.claim: Humanoid hardware and software are roughly 50/50 in importance, with hardware dominated by sensors, motor drives, and actuators and software tying it all together.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Neither pure-hardware nor pure-software plays capture the full stack; integrated system value is split.
insights.locator: Hardware and software are equally important

insights.claim: Figure AI holds numerous humanoid-related patents, many focused on movement and kinematics, positioning domestic cleaning as a desirable early use case.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Figure AI's movement/kinematics patent portfolio is an IP moat to watch in the humanoid race.
insights.locator: Once humanoids have full articulation

insights.claim: Humanoids have evolved from automated storage/retrieval systems to autonomous mobile robots, with general-purpose movement as the next phase enabled by edge AI processing.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Maturity ladder (ASRS to AMR to general-purpose) frames where each robotics vendor sits on the adoption curve.
insights.locator: Humanoid robots have evolved

themes.theme: Power efficiency as the binding constraint

insights.claim: Energy is framed as the biggest remaining problem for humanoids, because a robot that runs only 10 minutes before a battery swap is unusable, while other senses appear close to being solved.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Battery/power efficiency, not perception, is the gating constraint on humanoid deployment — a key bottleneck to monitor.
insights.quote: Energy is going to be the biggest problem.
insights.locator: The next challenge is efficiently powering robots

insights.claim: The biggest power consumption on a robot is generated by the motors, not the silicon, meaning actuator count and force requirements dominate battery drain.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Efficiency gains must target motors/actuators, not just chips — reframes where power-optimization value accrues.
insights.quote: Typically on such devices, the biggest power consumption will be generated by the motors, not by the silicon
insights.locator: For a robot, the battery power consumption

insights.claim: Low-power demand is driving customers to request lower-precision data types, which cut power, memory footprint, and chip size.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Low-precision compute demand shapes chip and memory design decisions for edge robotics and automotive.
insights.quote: Lower precision data types mean less power demanded, less memory, smaller memory systems, and smaller chips
insights.locator: The need for low power even affects

insights.claim: Automotive rejects both expensive cooling systems and battery-draining compute because range competition is critical, pushing demand for air-cooled, low-power chips that also suit mobile robotics.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Shared low-power/air-cooled requirements reinforce the automotive-robotics chip overlap and favor efficiency-first IP.
insights.locator: Power is equally critical in automotive

themes.theme: Physical-AI device boom and emerging senses

insights.claim: Physical AI is expected to trigger an explosion of edge devices over the next 5 to 10 years, with a much shorter and faster innovation cycle than prior technology waves.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: A physical-AI device boom analogous to the mobile-phone explosion implies rising edge-silicon volumes; watch for the 'killer app'.
insights.quote: In the next 5 to 10 years, we’ll see an explosion of devices shipping.
insights.locator: Just as the internet and new connectivity

insights.claim: MIPS is now a GlobalFoundries company, formerly owned by Synopsys.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Ownership shift of MIPS to GlobalFoundries signals foundry vertical integration into CPU IP for edge/physical AI.
insights.locator: said James Prior, head of marketing at MIPS

insights.claim: Smell is emerging as a future robotic sensing modality, with gas sensor arrays acting as olfactory receptors combined with neural networks trained on human-recognizable smells.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Robotic olfaction is an early-stage modality opening industrial safety and rescue use cases for gas-sensor makers.
insights.quote: a gas sensor array with varied sensitivity patterns could act as olfactory receptors
insights.locator: Looking ahead, as robots become more integrated

insights.claim: Harvard researchers built an e-nose using CPU-mounted mini fans, an MCU for AI/ML, and oxide-based sensors that can detect a wide range of gases to help find people trapped in dangerous locations for first responders.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Concrete e-nose prototype shows olfaction moving from concept toward deployable rescue/first-response applications.
insights.locator: At Harvard University’s School of Engineering

insights.claim: Boston Consulting Group warns that humanoid perception, dexterity, planning, and reasoning are improving at different speeds, and flashy demos can obscure which capabilities are actually mature versus experimental.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: Caution against extrapolating from demos — diligence on humanoid claims should separate mature from experimental capabilities.
insights.quote: highly visible demonstrations can obscure which capabilities are mature and which remain experimental
insights.locator: As noted in a recent Boston Consulting Group report
